Our content so far has been focused on how you can earn the trust of your counterpart, how you can reframe their mindset, and how you can influence their behavior. The purpose is not just to steer you toward your desired outcome, but to exceed it. We've immersed you in tactical empathy and trust-based influence strategies to accomplish this. The foundational message for you here is that tactical empathy has to be your precursor for everything you do. It makes every one of your negotiation strategies not only more effective, but also more efficient. There's no avoiding the fact that tactical empathy saves you time. Okay, so what happens if you find yourself in brass tacks, bare-knuckle bargaining, toe-to-toe stuff? This is where tactical empathy goes out the window, right? You have to be prepared to fight fire with fire, right? Wrong. First of all, let's deal with this fight-fire-with-fire idea. Fighting fire with fire leaves everything burned down. You ever hear of a fireman showing up at a fire carrying a flamethrower? Here's the backdrop of what we've advanced you to, where tactical empathy came from. Tactical empathy was originally designed for some of the toughest bargaining on the planet, kidnapping negotiations, ransom dollars for human life. Negotiators knowing who the counterpart was then is just as important as you knowing who the counterpart is now, whether in business or personal interactions. Remember, Carl Rogers said true progress can't be made until you accept a patient for who they are. Same notion works in negotiation. In hostage negotiation, the counterparts were commodities dealers, businessmen. To them, it was nothing more than a commodity exchange, another day on the job. They weren't terrorists, they weren't crazies or psychopaths. It's easy to put that label on that type of individual. However, it directly affects how you see and deal with them. Do you know who your counterparts really are? In kidnapping cases, the commodity happened to be people. They had a timeframe they wanted to hit and a rough dollar amount they had in mind. Sound familiar? At the end of the day, getting paid was what they wanted. And although the dollar amount was important, interestingly enough, the timeframe was even more important. That's where your leverage lies. They take what they could as long as they felt like they got the best deal they could. The key word here is felt. Not that in fact they got as much as they could, but they felt like they did as good as they could have. You know many times tactical empathy backfired? Never. No downside. No, I'm not saying they made every deal. I am saying it made every interaction tilt as much as possible in the direction of those negotiators. And they were never hurt by a tactical empathy approach. I'm also saying they got the best of a lot of really tough negotiators in ways they never would have without it. Now let's talk about the application to your world. We want to prepare you for the dynamic that includes hard bargaining. You'll eventually be faced with the tough bargainer if you haven't already. Let's say you've mirrored and labeled your way to a good degree of rapport, an accusations audit has cleared any residual mental or emotional obstacles, and you've identified and summarized the interests and positions at stake. You've triggered a that's right and still the bargaining remains. If you're like many people, this turns into an uneasy sequence of offers and counter offers. You break into a cold sweat or get a frog in your throat. If you view this part of the process as painful yet necessary evil, there's a high chance you'll get bested by those who have learned to embrace detention. The tough bargainer is going to be pretty good at simply rejecting your offer and making no counter offer to see if you'll bid against yourself. Bid against yourself once and you know what you've taught them, you'll do it again. Bargaining induces more anxiety and triggers more unfocused aggression than any other part of the process. This is where deals literally fall apart. It may be the most often fumbled and mishandled part of the interaction, and it will certainly be where you second guess yourself the most after the fact. I'm going to tell you the same thing you've heard several other times. You have to be willing to step outside your comfort zone. Embrace the awkward and discomfort that often arises during a negotiation. Even with the best laid plan in the world, many people's fears and frustrations grab them when they get to this moment. If you remember at the end of the last course, we mentioned the key to closing a deal is for you to keep working the process until the very end, until the very end. And really, this is just you staying nimble. You're in position to put points on the scoreboard. This is where the perception of pressure mounts and the negative emotions steer you off your game. In this course, you'll hear the tactics that will give you the edge in the bargaining process. You'll learn how psychological dynamics determine which tactics you should use and how you should implement them. To accomplish this, you're also going to examine the three broad types of negotiator personalities and what best tools you'll use for dealing with them look like. Now, of course, each person you deal with is unique, just like every fingerprint is unique. Yet, do you know how many types of fingerprints there are? Just three. Just like all of our skills and tactics, you'll have to practice them in order to master them. And just like all of our skills and tactics, they'll be awkward for you at first. Be grateful for awkwardness. It means you're learning. Repetition is the mother of skill. Bargaining is human nature, and you ignore human nature at your peril. In order for you to be able to bargain with the best of them, you'll learn to recognize the subtle human nature aspects that play vital roles at the bargaining table. As you become a truly skilled bargainer, you won't limit your thinking to just two dimensions of opening offers, high and low, counter offers, and closing moves. Once you've mastered the ability to identify these currents, these human nature aspects, you'll be able to read bargaining situations more accurately. You'll confidently answer the tactical questions that stump the less skilled negotiators, and you'll be ready for the bare-knuckle bargaining. And the best part is, the other side will never see it coming. Take your price and have them be happy to give it to you.
